is a [biological macromolecule](/wiki/Biological_macromolecule "Biological macromolecule"), usually a [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), that acts as a biological [catalyst](/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst"), accelerating [chemical reactions](/wiki/Chemical_reactions "Chemical reactions") without being consumed in the process. The [molecules](/wiki/Molecules "Molecules") on which enzymes act are called [substrates](/wiki/Substrate_(chemistry) "Substrate (chemistry)"), which are converted into [products](/wiki/Product_(chemistry) "Product (chemistry)"). Nearly all [metabolic processes](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") within a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") depend on [enzyme catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis") to occur at biologically relevant rates.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.1 A [metabolic pathway](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway") is typically composed of a series of enzyme-catalyzed steps. The study of enzymes is known as *enzymology*, and a related field focuses on [pseudoenzymes](/wiki/Pseudoenzyme "Pseudoenzyme")—proteins that have lost catalytic activity but may retain regulatory or scaffolding functions, often indicated by alterations in their [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") sequences or unusual 'pseudocatalytic' behavior.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-pmid24107129-3)

An **enzyme** is a [biological macromolecule](/wiki/Biological_macromolecule "Biological macromolecule"), usually a [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), that acts as a biological [catalyst](/wiki/Catalyst "Catalyst"), accelerating [chemical reactions](/wiki/Chemical_reactions "Chemical reactions") without being consumed in the process. The [molecules](/wiki/Molecules "Molecules") on which enzymes act are called [substrates](/wiki/Substrate_(chemistry) "Substrate (chemistry)"), which are converted into [products](/wiki/Product_(chemistry) "Product (chemistry)"). Nearly all [metabolic processes](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") within a [cell](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") depend on [enzyme catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis") to occur at biologically relevant rates.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.1 A [metabolic pathway](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway") is typically composed of a series of enzyme-catalyzed steps. The study of enzymes is known as *enzymology*, and a related field focuses on [pseudoenzymes](/wiki/Pseudoenzyme "Pseudoenzyme")—proteins that have lost catalytic activity but may retain regulatory or scaffolding functions, often indicated by alterations in their [amino acid](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") sequences or unusual 'pseudocatalytic' behavior.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-pmid24107129-3)

Enzymes are known to catalyze over 5,000 types of biochemical reactions.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Other biological catalysts include [catalytic RNA molecules](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme"), or [ribozymes](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme"), which are sometimes classified as enzymes despite being composed of RNA rather than protein. More recently, [biomolecular condensates](/wiki/Biomolecular_condensate "Biomolecular condensate") have been recognized as a third category of biocatalysts, capable of catalyzing reactions by creating interfaces and gradients—such as ionic gradients—that drive biochemical processes, even when their component proteins are not intrinsically catalytic.[[5]](#cite_note-Ball-2025-01-21-5)

Enzymes increase the [reaction rate](/wiki/Reaction_rate "Reaction rate") by lowering a reaction's [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy"), often by factors of millions. A striking example is [orotidine 5′-phosphate decarboxylase](/wiki/Orotidine_5%E2%80%B2-phosphate_decarboxylase "Orotidine 5′-phosphate decarboxylase"), which accelerates a reaction that would otherwise take millions of years to occur in milliseconds.[[6]](#cite_note-radzicka-6)[[7]](#cite_note-pmid17889251-7) Like all catalysts, enzymes do not affect the overall [equilibrium](/wiki/Chemical_equilibrium "Chemical equilibrium") of a reaction and are regenerated at the end of each cycle. What distinguishes them is their high specificity, determined by their unique [three-dimensional structure](/wiki/Tertiary_structure "Tertiary structure"), and their sensitivity to factors such as [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature") and [pH](/wiki/PH "PH"). Enzyme activity can be enhanced by [activators](/wiki/Enzyme_activator "Enzyme activator") or diminished by [inhibitors](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor"), many of which serve as [drugs](/wiki/Drug "Drug") or [poisons](/wiki/Poison "Poison"). Outside optimal conditions, enzymes may lose their structure through [denaturation](/wiki/Denaturation_(biochemistry) "Denaturation (biochemistry)"), leading to loss of function.

Enzymes have widespread practical applications. In industry, they are used to catalyze the production of [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotic "Antibiotic") and other complex molecules. In everyday life, enzymes in [biological washing powders](/wiki/Detergent_enzymes "Detergent enzymes") break down protein, starch, and [fat](/wiki/Fat "Fat") stains, enhancing cleaning performance. [Papain](/wiki/Papain "Papain") and other proteolytic enzymes are used in [meat tenderizers](/wiki/Meat_tenderizer "Meat tenderizer") to hydrolyze proteins, improving texture and digestibility. Their specificity and efficiency make enzymes indispensable in both biological systems and commercial processes.

## Etymology and history

By the late 17th and early 18th centuries, the digestion of [meat](/wiki/Meat "Meat") by stomach secretions[[8]](#cite_note-Reaumur1752-8) and the conversion of [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch") to [sugars](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar") by plant extracts and [saliva](/wiki/Saliva "Saliva") were known but the mechanisms by which these occurred had not been identified.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

French chemist [Anselme Payen](/wiki/Anselme_Payen "Anselme Payen") was the first to discover an enzyme, [diastase](/wiki/Diastase "Diastase"), in 1833.[[10]](#cite_note-10) A few decades later, when studying the [fermentation](/wiki/Fermentation_(food) "Fermentation (food)") of sugar to [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") by [yeast](/wiki/Yeast "Yeast"), [Louis Pasteur](/wiki/Louis_Pasteur "Louis Pasteur") concluded that this fermentation was caused by a [vital force](/wiki/Vital_force "Vital force") contained within the yeast cells called "ferments", which were thought to function only within living organisms. He wrote that "alcoholic fermentation is an act correlated with the life and organization of the yeast cells, not with the death or putrefaction of the cells."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1877, German physiologist [Wilhelm Kühne](/wiki/Wilhelm_K%C3%BChne "Wilhelm Kühne") (1837–1900) first used the term *[enzyme](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/enzyme "wiktionary:enzyme")*, which comes from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language") [ἔνζυμον](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%AD%CE%BD%CE%B6%CF%85%CE%BC%CE%BF "wikt:ένζυμο") *(énzymon)* '[leavened](/wiki/Bread#Leavening "Bread"), in yeast', to describe this process.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The word *enzyme* was used later to refer to nonliving substances such as [pepsin](/wiki/Pepsin "Pepsin"), and the word *ferment* was used to refer to chemical activity produced by living organisms.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![Photograph of Eduard Buchner.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Eduardbuchner.jpg/250px-Eduardbuchner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eduardbuchner.jpg)

Eduard Buchner

[Eduard Buchner](/wiki/Eduard_Buchner "Eduard Buchner") submitted his first paper on the study of yeast extracts in 1897. In a series of experiments at the [University of Berlin](/wiki/Humboldt_University_of_Berlin "Humboldt University of Berlin"), he found that sugar was fermented by yeast extracts even when there were no living yeast cells in the mixture.[[14]](#cite_note-urlEduard_Buchner_–_Biographical-14) He named the enzyme that brought about the fermentation of sucrose "[zymase](/wiki/Zymase "Zymase")".[[15]](#cite_note-urlEduard_Buchner_–_Nobel_Lecture:_Cell-Free_Fermentation-15) In 1907, he received the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") for "his discovery of cell-free fermentation". Following Buchner's example, enzymes are usually named according to the reaction they carry out: the suffix *[-ase](/wiki/-ase "-ase")* is combined with the name of the [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(biochemistry) "Substrate (biochemistry)") (e.g., [lactase](/wiki/Lactase "Lactase") is the enzyme that cleaves [lactose](/wiki/Lactose "Lactose")) or to the type of reaction (e.g., [DNA polymerase](/wiki/DNA_polymerase "DNA polymerase") forms DNA polymers).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The biochemical identity of enzymes was still unknown in the early 1900s. Many scientists observed that enzymatic activity was associated with proteins, but others (such as Nobel laureate [Richard Willstätter](/wiki/Richard_Willst%C3%A4tter "Richard Willstätter")) argued that proteins were merely carriers for the true enzymes and that proteins *per se* were incapable of catalysis.[[17]](#cite_note-Willstätter_1927-17) In 1926, [James B. Sumner](/wiki/James_B._Sumner "James B. Sumner") showed that the enzyme [urease](/wiki/Urease "Urease") was a pure protein and crystallized it; he did likewise for the enzyme [catalase](/wiki/Catalase "Catalase") in 1937. The conclusion that pure proteins can be enzymes was definitively demonstrated by [John Howard Northrop](/wiki/John_Howard_Northrop "John Howard Northrop") and [Wendell Meredith Stanley](/wiki/Wendell_Meredith_Stanley "Wendell Meredith Stanley"), who worked on the digestive enzymes [pepsin](/wiki/Pepsin "Pepsin") (1930), [trypsin](/wiki/Trypsin "Trypsin") and [chymotrypsin](/wiki/Chymotrypsin "Chymotrypsin"). These three scientists were awarded the 1946 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.[[18]](#cite_note-urlThe_Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry_1946-18)

The discovery that enzymes could be crystallized eventually allowed their structures to be solved by [x-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography"). This was first done for [lysozyme](/wiki/Lysozyme "Lysozyme"), an enzyme found in tears, saliva and [egg whites](/wiki/Egg_white "Egg white") that digests the coating of some bacteria; the structure was solved by a group led by [David Chilton Phillips](/wiki/David_Chilton_Phillips "David Chilton Phillips") and published in 1965.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This high-resolution structure of lysozyme marked the beginning of the field of [structural biology](/wiki/Structural_biology "Structural biology") and the effort to understand how enzymes work at an atomic level of detail.[[20]](#cite_note-pmid10390620-20)

## Classification and nomenclature

Enzymes can be classified by two main criteria: either [amino acid sequence](/wiki/Protein_primary_structure "Protein primary structure") similarity (and thus evolutionary relationship) or enzymatic activity.

**Enzyme activity**. An enzyme's name is often derived from its substrate or the chemical reaction it catalyzes, with the word ending in *-ase*.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.1.3 Examples are [lactase](/wiki/Lactase "Lactase"), [alcohol dehydrogenase](/wiki/Alcohol_dehydrogenase "Alcohol dehydrogenase") and [DNA polymerase](/wiki/DNA_polymerase "DNA polymerase"). Different enzymes that catalyze the same chemical reaction are called [isozymes](/wiki/Isozyme "Isozyme").[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 10.3

The [International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology](/wiki/International_Union_of_Biochemistry_and_Molecular_Biology "International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology") have developed a [nomenclature](/wiki/Nomenclature "Nomenclature") for enzymes, the [EC numbers (for "Enzyme Commission")](/wiki/Enzyme_Commission_number "Enzyme Commission number"). Each enzyme is described by "EC" followed by a sequence of four numbers which represent the hierarchy of enzymatic activity (from very general to very specific). That is, the first number broadly classifies the enzyme based on its mechanism while the other digits add more and more specificity.[[21]](#cite_note-moss-21)

The top-level classification is:

* EC 1, [Oxidoreductases](/wiki/Oxidoreductase "Oxidoreductase"): catalyze [oxidation](/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation")/reduction reactions
* EC 2, [Transferases](/wiki/Transferase "Transferase"): transfer a [functional group](/wiki/Functional_group "Functional group") (*e.g.* a methyl or phosphate group)
* EC 3, [Hydrolases](/wiki/Hydrolase "Hydrolase"): catalyze the [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") of various bonds
* EC 4, [Lyases](/wiki/Lyase "Lyase"): cleave various bonds by means other than hydrolysis and oxidation
* EC 5, [Isomerases](/wiki/Isomerase "Isomerase"): catalyze [isomerization](/wiki/Isomer "Isomer") changes within a single molecule
* EC 6, [Ligases](/wiki/Ligase "Ligase"): join two molecules with [covalent bonds](/wiki/Covalent_bond "Covalent bond").
* EC 7, [Translocases](/wiki/Translocase "Translocase"): catalyze the movement of ions or molecules across membranes, or their separation within membranes.

These sections are subdivided by other features such as the substrate, products, and [chemical mechanism](/wiki/Chemical_mechanism "Chemical mechanism"). An enzyme is fully specified by four numerical designations. For example, [hexokinase](/wiki/Hexokinase "Hexokinase") (EC 2.7.1.1) is a transferase (EC 2) that adds a phosphate group (EC 2.7) to a hexose sugar, a molecule containing an alcohol group (EC 2.7.1).[[22]](#cite_note-22)

**Sequence similarity**. EC categories do **not** reflect sequence similarity. For instance, two ligases of the same EC number that catalyze exactly the same reaction can have completely different sequences. Independent of their function, enzymes, like any other proteins, have been classified by their sequence similarity into numerous families. These families have been documented in dozens of different protein and protein family databases such as [Pfam](/wiki/Pfam "Pfam").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

**Non-homologous isofunctional enzymes**. Unrelated enzymes that have the same enzymatic activity have been called *non-homologous isofunctional enzymes*.[[24]](#cite_note-24) [Horizontal gene transfer](/wiki/Horizontal_gene_transfer "Horizontal gene transfer") may spread these genes to unrelated species, especially bacteria where they can replace endogenous genes of the same function, leading to hon-homologous gene displacement.

## Structure

[![A graph showing that reaction rate increases exponentially with temperature until denaturation causes it to decrease again.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Q10_graph_c.svg/500px-Q10_graph_c.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Q10_graph_c.svg)

Enzyme activity initially increases with temperature ([Q10 coefficient](/wiki/Q10_(temperature_coefficient) "Q10 (temperature coefficient)")) until the enzyme's structure unfolds ([denaturation](/wiki/Denaturation_(biochemistry) "Denaturation (biochemistry)")), leading to an optimal [rate of reaction](/wiki/Rate_of_reaction "Rate of reaction") at an intermediate temperature.

See also: [Protein structure](/wiki/Protein_structure "Protein structure")

Enzymes are generally [globular proteins](/wiki/Globular_protein "Globular protein"), acting alone or in larger [complexes](/wiki/Protein_complex "Protein complex"). The sequence of the amino acids specifies the structure which in turn determines the catalytic activity of the enzyme.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Although structure determines function, a novel enzymatic activity cannot yet be predicted from structure alone.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Enzyme structures unfold ([denature](/wiki/Denaturation_(biochemistry) "Denaturation (biochemistry)")) when heated or exposed to chemical denaturants and this disruption to the structure typically causes a loss of activity.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Enzyme denaturation is normally linked to temperatures above a species' normal level; as a result, enzymes from bacteria living in volcanic environments such as [hot springs](/wiki/Hot_spring "Hot spring") are prized by industrial users for their ability to function at high temperatures, allowing enzyme-catalysed reactions to be operated at a very high rate.

Enzymes are usually much larger than their substrates. Sizes range from just 62 amino acid residues, for the [monomer](/wiki/Monomer "Monomer") of [4-oxalocrotonate tautomerase](/wiki/4-Oxalocrotonate_tautomerase "4-Oxalocrotonate tautomerase"),[[28]](#cite_note-28) to over 2,500 residues in the animal [fatty acid synthase](/wiki/Fatty_acid_synthase "Fatty acid synthase").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Only a small portion of their structure (around 2–4 amino acids) is directly involved in catalysis: the catalytic site.[[30]](#cite_note-30) This catalytic site is located next to one or more [binding sites](/wiki/Binding_site "Binding site") where residues orient the substrates. The catalytic site and binding site together compose the enzyme's [active site](/wiki/Active_site "Active site"). The remaining majority of the enzyme structure serves to maintain the precise orientation and dynamics of the active site.[[31]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_7-31)

In some enzymes, no amino acids are directly involved in catalysis; instead, the enzyme contains sites to bind and orient catalytic [cofactors](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)").[[31]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_7-31) Enzyme structures may also contain [allosteric sites](/wiki/Allosteric_site "Allosteric site") where the binding of a small molecule causes a [conformational change](/wiki/Conformational_change "Conformational change") that increases or decreases activity.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

A small number of [RNA](/wiki/Ribonucleic_acid "Ribonucleic acid")-based biological catalysts called [ribozymes](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme") exist, which again can act alone or in complex with proteins. The most common of these is the [ribosome](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") which is a complex of protein and catalytic RNA components.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 2.2

## Mechanism

[![Lysozyme displayed as an opaque globular surface with a pronounced cleft which the substrate depicted as a stick diagram snuggly fits into.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Enzyme_structure.svg/500px-Enzyme_structure.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Enzyme_structure.svg)

Organisation of [enzyme structure](/wiki/Protein_structure "Protein structure") and [lysozyme](/wiki/Lysozyme "Lysozyme") example. Binding sites in blue, catalytic site in red and [peptidoglycan](/wiki/Peptidoglycan "Peptidoglycan") substrate in black. ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [9LYZ](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/9LYZ)​)

### Substrate binding

Enzymes must bind their substrates before they can catalyse any chemical reaction. Enzymes are usually very specific as to what [substrates](/wiki/Substrate_(biochemistry) "Substrate (biochemistry)") they bind and then the chemical reaction catalysed. [Specificity](/wiki/Chemical_specificity "Chemical specificity") is achieved by binding pockets with complementary shape, charge and [hydrophilic](/wiki/Hydrophilic "Hydrophilic")/[hydrophobic](/wiki/Hydrophobic "Hydrophobic") characteristics to the substrates. Enzymes can therefore distinguish between very similar substrate molecules to be [chemoselective](/wiki/Chemoselectivity "Chemoselectivity"), [regioselective](/wiki/Regioselectivity "Regioselectivity") and [stereospecific](/wiki/Stereospecificity "Stereospecificity").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Some of the enzymes showing the highest specificity and accuracy are involved in the copying and [expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") of the [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome"). Some of these enzymes have "[proof-reading](/wiki/Proofreading_(biology) "Proofreading (biology)")" mechanisms. Here, an enzyme such as [DNA polymerase](/wiki/DNA_polymerase "DNA polymerase") catalyzes a reaction in a first step and then checks that the product is correct in a second step.[[34]](#cite_note-34) This two-step process results in average error rates of less than 1 error in 100 million reactions in high-fidelity mammalian polymerases.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 5.3.1 Similar proofreading mechanisms are also found in [RNA polymerase](/wiki/RNA_polymerase "RNA polymerase"),[[35]](#cite_note-35) [aminoacyl tRNA synthetases](/wiki/Aminoacyl_tRNA_synthetase "Aminoacyl tRNA synthetase")[[36]](#cite_note-36) and [ribosomes](/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Conversely, some enzymes display [enzyme promiscuity](/wiki/Enzyme_promiscuity "Enzyme promiscuity"), having broad specificity and acting on a range of different physiologically relevant substrates. Many enzymes possess small side activities which arose fortuitously (i.e. [neutrally](/wiki/Neutral_evolution "Neutral evolution")), which may be the starting point for the evolutionary selection of a new function.[[38]](#cite_note-Tawfik10-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[![Hexokinase displayed as an opaque surface with a pronounced open binding cleft next to unbound substrate (top) and the same enzyme with more closed cleft that surrounds the bound substrate (bottom)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Hexokinase_induced_fit.svg/500px-Hexokinase_induced_fit.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hexokinase_induced_fit.svg)

Enzyme changes shape by induced fit upon substrate binding to form enzyme-substrate complex. [Hexokinase](/wiki/Hexokinase "Hexokinase") has a large induced fit motion that closes over the substrates [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") and [xylose](/wiki/Xylose "Xylose"). Binding sites in blue, substrates in black and [Mg2+](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") cofactor in yellow. ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [2E2N](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/2E2N)​, [2E2Q](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/2E2Q)​)

#### "Lock and key" model

To explain the observed specificity of enzymes, in 1894 [Emil Fischer](/wiki/Hermann_Emil_Fischer "Hermann Emil Fischer") proposed that both the enzyme and the substrate possess specific complementary geometric shapes that fit exactly into one another.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) This is often referred to as "the lock and key" model.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.3.2 This early model explains enzyme specificity, but fails to explain the stabilization of the transition state that enzymes achieve.[[42]](#cite_note-Cooper_2000-42)

#### Induced fit model

In 1958, [Daniel Koshland](/wiki/Daniel_E._Koshland,_Jr. "Daniel E. Koshland, Jr.") suggested a modification to the lock and key model: since enzymes are rather flexible structures, the active site is continuously reshaped by interactions with the substrate as the substrate interacts with the enzyme.[[43]](#cite_note-43) As a result, the substrate does not simply bind to a rigid active site; the amino acid [side-chains](/wiki/Side_chain "Side chain") that make up the active site are molded into the precise positions that enable the enzyme to perform its catalytic function. In some cases, such as [glycosidases](/wiki/Glycosidases "Glycosidases"), the substrate [molecule](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") also changes shape slightly as it enters the active site.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The active site continues to change until the substrate is completely bound, at which point the final shape and charge distribution is determined.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
Induced fit may enhance the fidelity of molecular recognition in the presence of competition and noise via the [conformational proofreading](/wiki/Conformational_proofreading "Conformational proofreading") mechanism.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Catalysis

See also: [Enzyme catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis") and [Transition state theory](/wiki/Transition_state_theory "Transition state theory")

Enzymes can accelerate reactions in several ways, all of which lower the [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") (ΔG‡, [Gibbs free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy"))[[47]](#cite_note-Fersht_1985-47)

1. By stabilizing the transition state:
   * Creating an environment with a charge distribution complementary to that of the transition state to lower its energy[[48]](#cite_note-48)
2. By providing an alternative reaction pathway:
   * Temporarily reacting with the substrate, forming a covalent intermediate to provide a lower energy transition state[[49]](#cite_note-49)
3. By destabilizing the substrate ground state:
   * Distorting bound substrate(s) into their transition state form to reduce the energy required to reach the transition state[[50]](#cite_note-PMID12947189-50)
   * By orienting the substrates into a productive arrangement to reduce the reaction [entropy](/wiki/Entropy "Entropy") change[[51]](#cite_note-51) (the contribution of this mechanism to catalysis is relatively small)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Enzymes may use several of these mechanisms simultaneously. For example, [proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") such as [trypsin](/wiki/Trypsin "Trypsin") perform covalent catalysis using a [catalytic triad](/wiki/Catalytic_triad "Catalytic triad"), stabilize charge build-up on the transition states using an [oxyanion hole](/wiki/Oxyanion_hole "Oxyanion hole"), complete [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") using an oriented water substrate.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Dynamics

See also: [Protein dynamics](/wiki/Protein_dynamics "Protein dynamics")

Enzymes are not rigid, static structures; instead they have complex internal dynamic motions – that is, movements of parts of the enzyme's structure such as individual amino acid residues, groups of residues forming a [protein loop](/wiki/Turn_(biochemistry) "Turn (biochemistry)") or unit of [secondary structure](/wiki/Protein_secondary_structure "Protein secondary structure"), or even an entire [protein domain](/wiki/Protein_domain "Protein domain"). These motions give rise to a [conformational ensemble](/wiki/Conformational_ensemble "Conformational ensemble") of slightly different structures that interconvert with one another at [equilibrium](/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium "Thermodynamic equilibrium"). Different states within this ensemble may be associated with different aspects of an enzyme's function. For example, different conformations of the enzyme [dihydrofolate reductase](/wiki/Dihydrofolate_reductase "Dihydrofolate reductase") are associated with the substrate binding, catalysis, cofactor release, and product release steps of the catalytic cycle,[[54]](#cite_note-54) consistent with [catalytic resonance theory](/wiki/Catalytic_resonance_theory "Catalytic resonance theory"). The transitions between the different conformations during the catalytic cycle involve internal [viscoelastic](/wiki/Viscoelasticity "Viscoelasticity") motion that is facilitated by high-[strain](/wiki/Strain_(mechanics) "Strain (mechanics)") regions where amino acids are rearranged.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Substrate presentation

[Substrate presentation](/wiki/Substrate_presentation "Substrate presentation") is a process where the enzyme is sequestered away from its substrate. Enzymes can be sequestered to the plasma membrane away from a substrate in the nucleus or cytosol.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Or within the membrane, an enzyme can be sequestered into lipid rafts away from its substrate in the disordered region. When the enzyme is released it mixes with its substrate. Alternatively, the enzyme can be sequestered near its substrate to activate the enzyme. For example, the enzyme can be soluble and upon activation bind to a lipid in the plasma membrane and then act upon molecules in the plasma membrane.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Allosteric modulation

Main article: [Allosteric regulation](/wiki/Allosteric_regulation "Allosteric regulation")

Allosteric sites are pockets on the enzyme, distinct from the active site, that bind to molecules in the cellular environment. These molecules then cause a change in the conformation or dynamics of the enzyme that is transduced to the active site and thus affects the reaction rate of the enzyme.[[58]](#cite_note-58) In this way, allosteric interactions can either inhibit or activate enzymes. Allosteric interactions with metabolites upstream or downstream in an enzyme's metabolic pathway cause [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") regulation, altering the activity of the enzyme according to the [flux](/wiki/Flux_(metabolism) "Flux (metabolism)") through the rest of the pathway.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Cofactors

[![Thiamine pyrophosphate displayed as an opaque globular surface with an open binding cleft where the substrate and cofactor both depicted as stick diagrams fit into.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Transketolase_%2B_TPP.png/500px-Transketolase_%2B_TPP.png)](/wiki/File:Transketolase_%2B_TPP.png)

Chemical structure for [thiamine pyrophosphate](/wiki/Thiamine_pyrophosphate "Thiamine pyrophosphate") and protein structure of [transketolase](/wiki/Transketolase "Transketolase"). Thiamine pyrophosphate cofactor in yellow and [xylulose 5-phosphate](/wiki/Xylulose_5-phosphate "Xylulose 5-phosphate") substrate in black. ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [4KXV](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/4KXV)​)

Main article: [Cofactor (biochemistry)](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)")

Some enzymes do not need additional components to show full activity. Others require non-protein molecules called cofactors to be bound for activity.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Cofactors can be either [inorganic](/wiki/Inorganic "Inorganic") (e.g., metal [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion") and [iron–sulfur clusters](/wiki/Iron%E2%80%93sulfur_cluster "Iron–sulfur cluster")) or [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound") (e.g., [flavin](/wiki/Flavin_group "Flavin group") and [heme](/wiki/Heme "Heme")). These cofactors serve many purposes; for instance, metal ions can help in stabilizing nucleophilic species within the active site.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Organic cofactors can be either [coenzymes](/wiki/Coenzyme "Coenzyme"), which are released from the enzyme's active site during the reaction, or [prosthetic groups](/wiki/Prosthetic_groups "Prosthetic groups"), which are tightly bound to an enzyme. Organic prosthetic groups can be covalently bound (e.g., [biotin](/wiki/Biotin "Biotin") in enzymes such as [pyruvate carboxylase](/wiki/Pyruvate_carboxylase "Pyruvate carboxylase")).[[62]](#cite_note-pmid10470036-62)

An example of an enzyme that contains a cofactor is [carbonic anhydrase](/wiki/Carbonic_anhydrase "Carbonic anhydrase"), which uses a zinc cofactor bound as part of its active site.[[63]](#cite_note-63) These tightly bound ions or molecules are usually found in the active site and are involved in catalysis.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.1.1 For example, flavin and heme cofactors are often involved in [redox](/wiki/Redox "Redox") reactions.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 17

Enzymes that require a cofactor but do not have one bound are called *apoenzymes* or *apoproteins*. An enzyme together with the cofactor(s) required for activity is called a *holoenzyme* (or haloenzyme). The term *holoenzyme* can also be applied to enzymes that contain multiple protein subunits, such as the [DNA polymerases](/wiki/DNA_polymerase "DNA polymerase"); here the holoenzyme is the complete complex containing all the subunits needed for activity.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.1.1

### Coenzymes

Coenzymes are small organic molecules that can be loosely or tightly bound to an enzyme. Coenzymes transport chemical groups from one enzyme to another.[[64]](#cite_note-Wagner_1975-64) Examples include [NADH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide"), [NADPH](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide_phosphate "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate") and [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP). Some coenzymes, such as [flavin mononucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_mononucleotide "Flavin mononucleotide") (FMN), [flavin adenine dinucleotide](/wiki/Flavin_adenine_dinucleotide "Flavin adenine dinucleotide") (FAD), [thiamine pyrophosphate](/wiki/Thiamine_pyrophosphate "Thiamine pyrophosphate") (TPP), and [tetrahydrofolate](/wiki/Tetrahydrofolate "Tetrahydrofolate") (THF), are derived from [vitamins](/wiki/Vitamin "Vitamin"). These coenzymes cannot be synthesized by the body *[de novo](/wiki/De_novo_synthesis "De novo synthesis")* and closely related compounds (vitamins) must be acquired from the diet. The chemical groups carried include:

* the [hydride](/wiki/Hydride "Hydride") ion (H−), carried by [NAD or NADP+](/wiki/Nicotinamide_adenine_dinucleotide "Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide")
* the phosphate group, carried by [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate")
* the acetyl group, carried by [coenzyme A](/wiki/Coenzyme_A "Coenzyme A")
* formyl, methenyl or methyl groups, carried by [folic acid](/wiki/Folic_acid "Folic acid") and
* the methyl group, carried by [S-adenosylmethionine](/wiki/S-adenosylmethionine "S-adenosylmethionine")[[64]](#cite_note-Wagner_1975-64)

Since coenzymes are chemically changed as a consequence of enzyme action, it is useful to consider coenzymes to be a special class of substrates, or second substrates, which are common to many different enzymes. For example, about 1000 enzymes are known to use the coenzyme NADH.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Coenzymes are usually continuously regenerated and their concentrations maintained at a steady level inside the cell. For example, NADPH is regenerated through the [pentose phosphate pathway](/wiki/Pentose_phosphate_pathway "Pentose phosphate pathway") and *S*-adenosylmethionine by [methionine adenosyltransferase](/wiki/Methionine_adenosyltransferase "Methionine adenosyltransferase"). This continuous regeneration means that small amounts of coenzymes can be used very intensively. For example, the human body turns over its own weight in ATP each day.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Thermodynamics

[![A two dimensional plot of reaction coordinate (x-axis) vs. energy (y-axis) for catalyzed and uncatalyzed reactions. The energy of the system steadily increases from reactants (x = 0) until a maximum is reached at the transition state (x = 0.5), and steadily decreases to the products (x = 1). However, in an enzyme catalysed reaction, binding generates an enzyme-substrate complex (with slightly reduced energy) then increases up to a transition state with a smaller maximum than the uncatalysed reaction.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Enzyme_catalysis_energy_levels_2.svg/500px-Enzyme_catalysis_energy_levels_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Enzyme_catalysis_energy_levels_2.svg)

The energies of the stages of a [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction"). Uncatalysed (dashed line), substrates need a lot of [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") to reach a [transition state](/wiki/Transition_state "Transition state"), which then decays into lower-energy products. When enzyme catalysed (solid line), the enzyme binds the substrates (ES), then stabilizes the transition state (ES‡) to reduce the activation energy required to produce products (EP) which are finally released.

Main articles: [Activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy"), [Thermodynamic equilibrium](/wiki/Thermodynamic_equilibrium "Thermodynamic equilibrium"), and [Chemical equilibrium](/wiki/Chemical_equilibrium "Chemical equilibrium")

As with all catalysts, enzymes do not alter the position of the chemical equilibrium of the reaction. In the presence of an enzyme, the reaction runs in the same direction as it would without the enzyme, just more quickly.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.2.3 For example, [carbonic anhydrase](/wiki/Carbonic_anhydrase "Carbonic anhydrase") catalyzes its reaction in either direction depending on the concentration of its reactants:[[67]](#cite_note-67)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle {\mathrm {CO} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}{}+{}\mathrm {H} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}\mathrm {O} {}\mathrel {\xrightarrow {\text{Carbonic anhydrase}} } {}\mathrm {H} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}\mathrm {CO} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{3}}}} (in [tissues](/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)"); high CO2 concentration) |  | 1 |

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle {\mathrm {CO} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}{}+{}\mathrm {H} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}\mathrm {O} {}\mathrel {\xleftarrow {\text{Carbonic anhydrase}} } {}\mathrm {H} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{2}}\mathrm {CO} {\vphantom {A}}_{\smash[{t}]{3}}}} (in [lungs](/wiki/Lung "Lung"); low CO2 concentration) |  | 2 |

The rate of a reaction is dependent on the [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") needed to form the [transition state](/wiki/Transition_state "Transition state") which then decays into products. Enzymes increase reaction rates by lowering the energy of the transition state. First, binding forms a low energy enzyme-substrate complex (ES). Second, the enzyme stabilises the transition state such that it requires less energy to achieve compared to the uncatalyzed reaction (ES‡). Finally the enzyme-product complex (EP) dissociates to release the products.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.3

Enzymes can couple two or more reactions, so that a thermodynamically favorable reaction can be used to "drive" a thermodynamically unfavourable one so that the combined energy of the products is lower than the substrates. For example, the hydrolysis of [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") is often used to drive other chemical reactions.[[68]](#cite_note-Nicholls-68)

## Kinetics

[![Schematic reaction diagrams for uncatalzyed (Substrate to Product) and catalyzed (Enzyme + Substrate to Enzyme/Substrate complex to Enzyme + Product)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Enzyme_mechanism_2.svg/330px-Enzyme_mechanism_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Enzyme_mechanism_2.svg)

A chemical reaction mechanism with or without [enzyme catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis"). The enzyme (E) binds [substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(chemistry) "Substrate (chemistry)") (S) to produce [product](/wiki/Product_(chemistry) "Product (chemistry)") (P).

[![A two dimensional plot of substrate concentration (x axis) vs. reaction rate (y axis). The shape of the curve is hyperbolic. The rate of the reaction is zero at zero concentration of substrate and the rate asymptotically reaches a maximum at high substrate concentration.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Michaelis_Menten_curve_2.svg/330px-Michaelis_Menten_curve_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Michaelis_Menten_curve_2.svg)

[Saturation curve](/wiki/Michaelis%E2%80%93Menten_kinetics "Michaelis–Menten kinetics") for an enzyme reaction showing the relation between the substrate concentration and reaction rate.

Main article: [Enzyme kinetics](/wiki/Enzyme_kinetics "Enzyme kinetics")

Enzyme kinetics is the investigation of how enzymes bind substrates and turn them into products.[[69]](#cite_note-69) The rate data used in kinetic analyses are commonly obtained from [enzyme assays](/wiki/Enzyme_assay "Enzyme assay"). In 1913 [Leonor Michaelis](/wiki/Leonor_Michaelis "Leonor Michaelis") and [Maud Leonora Menten](/wiki/Maud_Leonora_Menten "Maud Leonora Menten") proposed a quantitative theory of enzyme kinetics, which is referred to as [Michaelis–Menten kinetics](/wiki/Michaelis%E2%80%93Menten_kinetics "Michaelis–Menten kinetics").[[70]](#cite_note-70) The major contribution of Michaelis and Menten was to think of enzyme reactions in two stages. In the first, the substrate binds reversibly to the enzyme, forming the enzyme-substrate complex. This is sometimes called the Michaelis–Menten complex in their honor. The enzyme then catalyzes the chemical step in the reaction and releases the product. This work was further developed by [G. E. Briggs](/wiki/George_Edward_Briggs "George Edward Briggs") and [J. B. S. Haldane](/wiki/J._B._S._Haldane "J. B. S. Haldane"), who derived kinetic equations that are still widely used today.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Enzyme rates depend on [solution](/wiki/Solution_(chemistry) "Solution (chemistry)") conditions and substrate [concentration](/wiki/Concentration "Concentration"). To find the maximum speed of an enzymatic reaction, the substrate concentration is increased until a constant rate of product formation is seen. This is shown in the saturation curve on the right. Saturation happens because, as substrate concentration increases, more and more of the free enzyme is converted into the substrate-bound ES complex. At the maximum reaction rate (*V*max) of the enzyme, all the enzyme active sites are bound to substrate, and the amount of ES complex is the same as the total amount of enzyme.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.4

*V*max is only one of several important kinetic parameters. The amount of substrate needed to achieve a given rate of reaction is also important. This is given by the [Michaelis–Menten constant](/wiki/Michaelis%E2%80%93Menten_constant "Michaelis–Menten constant") (*K*m), which is the substrate concentration required for an enzyme to reach one-half its maximum reaction rate; generally, each enzyme has a characteristic *K*M for a given substrate. Another useful constant is *k*cat, also called the *turnover number*, which is the number of substrate molecules handled by one active site per second.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.4

The efficiency of an enzyme can be expressed in terms of *k*cat/*K*m. This is also called the specificity constant and incorporates the [rate constants](/wiki/Rate_constant "Rate constant") for all steps in the reaction up to and including the first irreversible step. Because the specificity constant reflects both affinity and catalytic ability, it is useful for comparing different enzymes against each other, or the same enzyme with different substrates. The theoretical maximum for the specificity constant is called the diffusion limit and is about 108 to 109 (M−1 s−1). At this point every collision of the enzyme with its substrate will result in catalysis, and the rate of product formation is not limited by the reaction rate but by the diffusion rate. Enzymes with this property are called *[catalytically perfect](/wiki/Catalytically_perfect_enzyme "Catalytically perfect enzyme")* or *kinetically perfect*. Example of such enzymes are [triose-phosphate isomerase](/wiki/Triosephosphateisomerase "Triosephosphateisomerase"), [carbonic anhydrase](/wiki/Carbonic_anhydrase "Carbonic anhydrase"), [acetylcholinesterase](/wiki/Acetylcholinesterase "Acetylcholinesterase"), [catalase](/wiki/Catalase "Catalase"), [fumarase](/wiki/Fumarase "Fumarase"), [β-lactamase](/wiki/%CE%92-lactamase "Β-lactamase"), and [superoxide dismutase](/wiki/Superoxide_dismutase "Superoxide dismutase").[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 8.4.2 The turnover of such enzymes can reach several million reactions per second.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 9.2 But most enzymes are far from perfect: the average values of ![{\displaystyle k_{\rm {cat}}/K_{\rm {m}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/20420b052126966d4009c65c5342cda33036e809) and ![{\displaystyle k_{\rm {cat}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/90626a789e6fd3e4651c36328c1fcfa64789b38f) are about ![{\displaystyle 10^{5}{\rm {s}}^{-1}{\rm {M}}^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ef512037b4da4d312f89dc4e35a5ca44cee2a187) and ![{\displaystyle 10{\rm {s}}^{-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/34cbb1e27dae0c04fc794a91f2aa001aca7054c1), respectively.[[72]](#cite_note-Bar-Even_2011-72)

Michaelis–Menten kinetics relies on the [law of mass action](/wiki/Law_of_mass_action "Law of mass action"), which is derived from the assumptions of free [diffusion](/wiki/Diffusion "Diffusion") and thermodynamically driven random collision. Many biochemical or cellular processes deviate significantly from these conditions, because of [macromolecular crowding](/wiki/Macromolecular_crowding "Macromolecular crowding") and constrained molecular movement.[[73]](#cite_note-73) More recent, complex extensions of the model attempt to correct for these effects.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

## Inhibition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/DHFR_methotrexate_inhibitor.svg/500px-DHFR_methotrexate_inhibitor.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DHFR_methotrexate_inhibitor.svg)

[![Two dimensional representations of the chemical structure of folic acid and methotrexate highlighting the differences between these two substances (amidation of pyrimidone and methylation of secondary amine).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Methotrexate_vs_folate_2.svg/500px-Methotrexate_vs_folate_2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Methotrexate_vs_folate_2.svg)

The coenzyme [folic acid](/wiki/Folic_acid "Folic acid") (left) and the anti-cancer drug [methotrexate](/wiki/Methotrexate "Methotrexate") (right) are very similar in structure (differences show in green). As a result, methotrexate is a competitive inhibitor of many enzymes that use folates.

Main article: [Enzyme inhibitor](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor")

Enzyme reaction rates can be decreased by various types of enzyme inhibitors.[[75]](#cite_note-Cornish-Bowden_2004-75): 73–74

### Types of inhibition

#### Competitive

A [competitive inhibitor](/wiki/Competitive_inhibitor "Competitive inhibitor") and substrate cannot bind to the enzyme at the same time.[[76]](#cite_note-Price_1979-76) Often competitive inhibitors strongly resemble the real substrate of the enzyme. For example, the drug [methotrexate](/wiki/Methotrexate "Methotrexate") is a competitive inhibitor of the enzyme [dihydrofolate reductase](/wiki/Dihydrofolate_reductase "Dihydrofolate reductase"), which catalyzes the reduction of [dihydrofolate](/wiki/Folic_acid "Folic acid") to tetrahydrofolate.[[77]](#cite_note-Goodsell_340–341-77) The similarity between the structures of dihydrofolate and this drug are shown in the accompanying figure. This type of inhibition can be overcome with high substrate concentration. In some cases, the inhibitor can bind to a site other than the binding-site of the usual substrate and exert an [allosteric effect](#Allosteric_modulation) to change the shape of the usual binding-site.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Non-competitive

A [non-competitive inhibitor](/wiki/Non-competitive_inhibition "Non-competitive inhibition") binds to a site other than where the substrate binds. The substrate still binds with its usual affinity and hence Km remains the same. However the inhibitor reduces the catalytic efficiency of the enzyme so that Vmax is reduced. In contrast to competitive inhibition, non-competitive inhibition cannot be overcome with high substrate concentration.[[75]](#cite_note-Cornish-Bowden_2004-75): 76–78

#### Uncompetitive

An [uncompetitive inhibitor](/wiki/Uncompetitive_inhibitor "Uncompetitive inhibitor") cannot bind to the free enzyme, only to the enzyme-substrate complex; hence, these types of inhibitors are most effective at high substrate concentration. In the presence of the inhibitor, the enzyme-substrate complex is inactive.[[75]](#cite_note-Cornish-Bowden_2004-75): 78 This type of inhibition is rare.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

#### Mixed

A [mixed inhibitor](/wiki/Mixed_inhibition "Mixed inhibition") binds to an allosteric site and the binding of the substrate and the inhibitor affect each other. The enzyme's function is reduced but not eliminated when bound to the inhibitor. This type of inhibitor does not follow the Michaelis–Menten equation.[[75]](#cite_note-Cornish-Bowden_2004-75): 76–78

#### Irreversible

An [irreversible inhibitor](/wiki/Irreversible_inhibitor "Irreversible inhibitor") permanently inactivates the enzyme, usually by forming a [covalent bond](/wiki/Covalent_bond "Covalent bond") to the protein.[[80]](#cite_note-80) [Penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin")[[81]](#cite_note-81) and [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin")[[82]](#cite_note-Johnson-82) are common drugs that act in this manner.

### Functions of inhibitors

In many organisms, inhibitors may act as part of a [feedback](/wiki/Feedback "Feedback") mechanism. If an enzyme produces too much of one substance in the organism, that substance may act as an inhibitor for the enzyme at the beginning of the pathway that produces it, causing production of the substance to slow down or stop when there is sufficient amount. This is a form of [negative feedback](/wiki/Negative_feedback "Negative feedback"). Major metabolic pathways such as the [citric acid cycle](/wiki/Citric_acid_cycle "Citric acid cycle") make use of this mechanism.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 17.2.2

Since inhibitors modulate the function of enzymes they are often used as drugs. Many such drugs are reversible competitive inhibitors that resemble the enzyme's native substrate, similar to [methotrexate](/wiki/Methotrexate "Methotrexate") above; other well-known examples include [statins](/wiki/Statin "Statin") used to treat high [cholesterol](/wiki/Cholesterol "Cholesterol"),[[83]](#cite_note-Endo1992-83) and [protease inhibitors](/wiki/Protease_inhibitors "Protease inhibitors") used to treat [retroviral](/wiki/Retroviral "Retroviral") infections such as [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV").[[84]](#cite_note-84) A common example of an irreversible inhibitor that is used as a drug is [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin"), which inhibits the [COX-1](/wiki/Cyclooxygenase "Cyclooxygenase") and [COX-2](/wiki/Cyclooxygenase "Cyclooxygenase") enzymes that produce the [inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation") messenger [prostaglandin](/wiki/Prostaglandin "Prostaglandin").[[82]](#cite_note-Johnson-82) Other enzyme inhibitors are poisons. For example, the poison [cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide "Cyanide") is an irreversible enzyme inhibitor that combines with the copper and iron in the active site of the enzyme [cytochrome c oxidase](/wiki/Cytochrome_c_oxidase "Cytochrome c oxidase") and blocks [cellular respiration](/wiki/Cellular_respiration "Cellular respiration").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Biological function

Enzymes serve a wide variety of [functions](/wiki/Function_(biology) "Function (biology)") inside living organisms. They are indispensable for [signal transduction](/wiki/Signal_transduction "Signal transduction") and cell regulation, often via [kinases](/wiki/Kinase "Kinase") and [phosphatases](/wiki/Phosphatase "Phosphatase").[[87]](#cite_note-87) They also generate movement, with [myosin](/wiki/Myosin "Myosin") hydrolyzing [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP) to generate [muscle contraction](/wiki/Muscle_contraction "Muscle contraction"), and also transport cargo around the cell as part of the [cytoskeleton](/wiki/Cytoskeleton "Cytoskeleton").[[88]](#cite_note-88) Other [ATPases](/wiki/ATPase "ATPase") in the cell membrane are [ion pumps](/wiki/Ion_pump_(biology) "Ion pump (biology)") involved in [active transport](/wiki/Active_transport "Active transport"). Enzymes are also involved in more exotic functions, such as [luciferase](/wiki/Luciferase "Luciferase") generating light in [fireflies](/wiki/Fireflies "Fireflies").[[89]](#cite_note-89) [Viruses](/wiki/Virus "Virus") can also contain enzymes for infecting cells, such as the [HIV integrase](/wiki/HIV_integrase "HIV integrase") and [reverse transcriptase](/wiki/Reverse_transcriptase "Reverse transcriptase"), or for viral release from cells, like the [influenza](/wiki/Influenza "Influenza") virus [neuraminidase](/wiki/Neuraminidase "Neuraminidase").[[90]](#cite_note-pmid12370077-90)

An important function of enzymes is in the [digestive systems](/wiki/Digestive_systems "Digestive systems") of animals. Enzymes such as [amylases](/wiki/Amylase "Amylase") and [proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") break down large molecules ([starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch") or [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein"), respectively) into smaller ones, so they can be absorbed by the intestines. Starch molecules, for example, are too large to be absorbed from the intestine, but enzymes hydrolyze the starch chains into smaller molecules such as [maltose](/wiki/Maltose "Maltose") and eventually [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose"), which can then be absorbed. Different enzymes digest different food substances. In [ruminants](/wiki/Ruminant "Ruminant"), which have [herbivorous](/wiki/Herbivorous "Herbivorous") diets, microorganisms in the gut produce another enzyme, [cellulase](/wiki/Cellulase "Cellulase"), to break down the cellulose cell walls of plant fiber.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Metabolism

[![Schematic diagram of the glycolytic metabolic pathway starting with glucose and ending with pyruvate via several intermediate chemicals. Each step in the pathway is catalyzed by a unique enzyme.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Glycolysis_metabolic_pathway.svg/500px-Glycolysis_metabolic_pathway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Glycolysis_metabolic_pathway.svg)

The [metabolic pathway](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway") of [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") releases energy by converting [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") to [pyruvate](/wiki/Pyruvate "Pyruvate") via a series of intermediate metabolites. Each chemical modification (red box) is performed by a different enzyme.

Several enzymes can work together in a specific order, creating [metabolic pathways](/wiki/Metabolic_pathway "Metabolic pathway").[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 30.1 In a metabolic pathway, one enzyme takes the product of another enzyme as a substrate. After the catalytic reaction, the product is then passed on to another enzyme. Sometimes more than one enzyme can catalyze the same reaction in parallel; this can allow more complex regulation: with, for example, a low constant activity provided by one enzyme but an inducible high activity from a second enzyme.[[92]](#cite_note-Rouzer_2009-92)

Enzymes determine what steps occur in these pathways. Without enzymes, metabolism would neither progress through the same steps and could not be regulated to serve the needs of the cell. Most central metabolic pathways are regulated at a few steps, typically through enzymes whose activity involves the phosphorylation by ATP. Because this reaction releases so much energy, other reactions that are [thermodynamically unfavorable](/wiki/Endothermic "Endothermic") can be coupled to ATP hydrolysis, driving the overall series of linked metabolic reactions.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 30.1

### Control of activity

There are five main ways that enzyme activity is controlled in the cell.[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 30.1.1

#### Regulation

Enzymes can be either [activated](/wiki/Enzyme_activator "Enzyme activator") or [inhibited](/wiki/Enzyme_inhibitor "Enzyme inhibitor") by other molecules. For example, the end product(s) of a metabolic pathway are often inhibitors for one of the first enzymes of the pathway (usually the first irreversible step, called committed step), thus regulating the amount of end product made by the pathways. Such a regulatory mechanism is called a [negative feedback mechanism](/wiki/Negative_feedback "Negative feedback"), because the amount of the end product produced is regulated by its own concentration.[[93]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_8-93): 141–48 Negative feedback mechanism can effectively adjust the rate of synthesis of intermediate metabolites according to the demands of the cells. This helps with effective allocations of materials and energy economy, and it prevents the excess manufacture of end products. Like other [homeostatic devices](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis"), the control of enzymatic action helps to maintain a stable internal environment in living organisms.[[93]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_8-93): 141

#### Post-translational modification

Examples of [post-translational modification](/wiki/Post-translational_modification "Post-translational modification") include [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation"), [myristoylation](/wiki/Myristoylation "Myristoylation") and [glycosylation](/wiki/Glycosylation "Glycosylation").[[93]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_8-93): 149–69 For example, in the response to [insulin](/wiki/Insulin "Insulin"), the [phosphorylation](/wiki/Phosphorylation "Phosphorylation") of multiple enzymes, including [glycogen synthase](/wiki/Glycogen_synthase "Glycogen synthase"), helps control the synthesis or degradation of [glycogen](/wiki/Glycogen "Glycogen") and allows the cell to respond to changes in [blood sugar](/wiki/Blood_sugar "Blood sugar").[[94]](#cite_note-Doble_2003-94) Another example of post-translational modification is the cleavage of the polypeptide chain. [Chymotrypsin](/wiki/Chymotrypsin "Chymotrypsin"), a digestive protease, is produced in inactive form as [chymotrypsinogen](/wiki/Chymotrypsinogen "Chymotrypsinogen") in the [pancreas](/wiki/Pancreas "Pancreas") and transported in this form to the [stomach](/wiki/Stomach "Stomach") where it is activated. This stops the enzyme from digesting the pancreas or other tissues before it enters the gut. This type of inactive precursor to an enzyme is known as a [zymogen](/wiki/Zymogen "Zymogen")[[93]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_8-93): 149–53 or proenzyme.

#### Quantity

Enzyme production ([transcription](/wiki/Transcription_(genetics) "Transcription (genetics)") and [translation](/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") of enzyme genes) can be enhanced or diminished by a cell in response to changes in the cell's environment. This form of [gene regulation](/wiki/Regulation_of_gene_expression "Regulation of gene expression") is called [enzyme induction](/wiki/Enzyme_induction "Enzyme induction"). For example, bacteria may become [resistant to antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotic_resistance "Antibiotic resistance") such as [penicillin](/wiki/Penicillin "Penicillin") because enzymes called [beta-lactamases](/wiki/Beta-lactamase "Beta-lactamase") are induced that hydrolyse the crucial [beta-lactam ring](/wiki/Beta-lactam "Beta-lactam") within the penicillin molecule.[[95]](#cite_note-pmid8452343-95) Another example comes from enzymes in the [liver](/wiki/Liver "Liver") called [cytochrome P450 oxidases](/wiki/Cytochrome_P450_oxidase "Cytochrome P450 oxidase"), which are important in [drug metabolism](/wiki/Drug_metabolism "Drug metabolism"). Induction or inhibition of these enzymes can cause [drug interactions](/wiki/Drug_interaction "Drug interaction").[[96]](#cite_note-Skett_Gibson_2001-96) Enzyme levels can also be regulated by changing the rate of enzyme [degradation](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism").[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 30.1.1 The opposite of enzyme induction is [enzyme repression](/wiki/Enzyme_repression "Enzyme repression").

#### Subcellular distribution

Enzymes can be compartmentalized, with different metabolic pathways occurring in different [cellular compartments](/wiki/Cellular_compartment "Cellular compartment"). For example, [fatty acids](/wiki/Fatty_acid "Fatty acid") are synthesized by one set of enzymes in the [cytosol](/wiki/Cytosol "Cytosol"), [endoplasmic reticulum](/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum") and [Golgi](/wiki/Golgi_apparatus "Golgi apparatus") and used by a different set of enzymes as a source of energy in the [mitochondrion](/wiki/Mitochondrion "Mitochondrion"), through [β-oxidation](/wiki/%CE%92-oxidation "Β-oxidation").[[97]](#cite_note-97) In addition, [trafficking](/wiki/Protein_targeting "Protein targeting") of the enzyme to different compartments may change the degree of [protonation](/wiki/Protonation "Protonation") (e.g., the neutral [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm") and the acidic [lysosome](/wiki/Lysosome "Lysosome")) or oxidative state (e.g., oxidizing [periplasm](/wiki/Periplasm "Periplasm") or reducing [cytoplasm](/wiki/Cytoplasm "Cytoplasm")) which in turn affects enzyme activity.[[98]](#cite_note-Suzuki_2015_4-98) In contrast to partitioning into membrane bound organelles, enzyme subcellular localisation may also be altered through polymerisation of enzymes into macromolecular cytoplasmic filaments.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

#### Organ specialization

In [multicellular](/wiki/Multicellular "Multicellular") [eukaryotes](/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote"), cells in different [organs](/wiki/Organ_(anatomy) "Organ (anatomy)") and [tissues](/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)") have different patterns of [gene expression](/wiki/Gene_expression "Gene expression") and therefore have different sets of enzymes (known as [isozymes](/wiki/Isozyme "Isozyme")) available for metabolic reactions. This provides a mechanism for regulating the overall metabolism of the organism. For example, [hexokinase](/wiki/Hexokinase "Hexokinase"), the first enzyme in the [glycolysis](/wiki/Glycolysis "Glycolysis") pathway, has a specialized form called [glucokinase](/wiki/Glucokinase "Glucokinase") expressed in the liver and [pancreas](/wiki/Pancreas "Pancreas") that has a lower [affinity](/wiki/Affinity_(pharmacology) "Affinity (pharmacology)") for glucose yet is more sensitive to glucose concentration.[[101]](#cite_note-101) This enzyme is involved in sensing [blood sugar](/wiki/Blood_sugar "Blood sugar") and regulating insulin production.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

### Involvement in disease

[![Ribbon diagram of phenylalanine hydroxylase with bound cofactor, coenzyme and substrate](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Phenylalanine_hydroxylase_mutations.svg/500px-Phenylalanine_hydroxylase_mutations.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Phenylalanine_hydroxylase_mutations.svg)

In [phenylalanine hydroxylase](/wiki/Phenylalanine_hydroxylase "Phenylalanine hydroxylase") over 300 different mutations throughout the structure cause [phenylketonuria](/wiki/Phenylketonuria "Phenylketonuria"). [Phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine") substrate and [tetrahydrobiopterin](/wiki/Tetrahydrobiopterin "Tetrahydrobiopterin") coenzyme in black, and [Fe2+](/wiki/Iron "Iron") cofactor in yellow. ([PDB](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"): [1KW0](https://www.rcsb.org/structure/1KW0)​)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Autosomal_recessive_inheritance_for_affected_enzyme.png/500px-Autosomal_recessive_inheritance_for_affected_enzyme.png)](/wiki/File:Autosomal_recessive_inheritance_for_affected_enzyme.png)

Hereditary defects in enzymes are generally inherited in an [autosomal](/wiki/Autosomal_inheritance "Autosomal inheritance") fashion because there are more non-X chromosomes than X-chromosomes, and a [recessive](/wiki/Recessive_inheritance "Recessive inheritance") fashion because the enzymes from the unaffected genes are generally sufficient to prevent symptoms in carriers.

See also: [Genetic disorder](/wiki/Genetic_disorder "Genetic disorder")

Since the tight control of enzyme activity is essential for [homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis"), any malfunction (mutation, overproduction, underproduction or deletion) of a single critical enzyme can lead to a genetic disease. The malfunction of just one type of enzyme out of the thousands of types present in the human body can be fatal. An example of a fatal genetic disease due to enzyme insufficiency is [Tay–Sachs disease](/wiki/Tay%E2%80%93Sachs_disease "Tay–Sachs disease"), in which patients lack the enzyme [hexosaminidase](/wiki/Hexosaminidase "Hexosaminidase").[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)

One example of enzyme deficiency is the most common type of [phenylketonuria](/wiki/Phenylketonuria "Phenylketonuria"). Many different single amino acid mutations in the enzyme [phenylalanine hydroxylase](/wiki/Phenylalanine_hydroxylase "Phenylalanine hydroxylase"), which catalyzes the first step in the degradation of [phenylalanine](/wiki/Phenylalanine "Phenylalanine"), result in build-up of phenylalanine and related products. Some mutations are in the active site, directly disrupting binding and catalysis, but many are far from the active site and reduce activity by destabilising the protein structure, or affecting correct oligomerisation.[[105]](#cite_note-pmid10527663-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) This can lead to [intellectual disability](/wiki/Intellectual_disability "Intellectual disability") if the disease is untreated.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Another example is [pseudocholinesterase deficiency](/wiki/Pseudocholinesterase_deficiency "Pseudocholinesterase deficiency"), in which the body's ability to break down choline ester drugs is impaired.[[108]](#cite_note-108)
Oral administration of enzymes can be used to treat some functional enzyme deficiencies, such as [pancreatic insufficiency](/wiki/Pancreatic_insufficiency "Pancreatic insufficiency")[[109]](#cite_note-109) and [lactose intolerance](/wiki/Lactose_intolerance "Lactose intolerance").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

Another way enzyme malfunctions can cause disease comes from [germline mutations](/wiki/Germline_mutation "Germline mutation") in genes coding for [DNA repair](/wiki/DNA_repair "DNA repair") enzymes. Defects in these enzymes cause cancer because cells are less able to repair mutations in their [genomes](/wiki/Genome "Genome"). This causes a slow accumulation of mutations and results in the [development of cancers](/wiki/Carcinogenesis "Carcinogenesis"). An example of such a hereditary [cancer syndrome](/wiki/Cancer_syndrome "Cancer syndrome") is [xeroderma pigmentosum](/wiki/Xeroderma_pigmentosum "Xeroderma pigmentosum"), which causes the development of [skin cancers](/wiki/Skin_cancer "Skin cancer") in response to even minimal exposure to [ultraviolet light](/wiki/Ultraviolet_light "Ultraviolet light").[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-Andrews-112)

## Evolution

Similar to any other protein, enzymes change over time through [mutations](/wiki/Mutation "Mutation") and sequence divergence. Given their central role in [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism"), enzyme evolution plays a critical role in [adaptation](/wiki/Adaptation "Adaptation"). A key question is therefore whether and how enzymes can change their enzymatic activities alongside. It is generally accepted that many new enzyme activities have evolved through [gene duplication](/wiki/Gene_duplication "Gene duplication") and mutation of the duplicate copies although evolution can also happen without duplication. One example of an enzyme that has changed its activity is the ancestor of [methionyl aminopeptidase](/wiki/Methionyl_aminopeptidase "Methionyl aminopeptidase") (MAP) and creatine amidinohydrolase ([creatinase](/wiki/Creatinase "Creatinase")) which are clearly homologous but catalyze very different reactions (MAP removes the amino-terminal [methionine](/wiki/Methionine "Methionine") in new proteins while creatinase hydrolyses [creatine](/wiki/Creatine "Creatine") to [sarcosine](/wiki/Sarcosine "Sarcosine") and [urea](/wiki/Urea "Urea")). In addition, MAP is metal-ion dependent while creatinase is not, hence this property was also lost over time.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Small changes of enzymatic activity are extremely common among enzymes. In particular, substrate binding specificity (see above) can easily and quickly change with single amino acid changes in their substrate binding pockets. This is frequently seen in the main enzyme classes such as [kinases](/wiki/Kinase "Kinase").[[114]](#cite_note-114)

Artificial (in vitro) evolution is now commonly used to modify enzyme activity or specificity for industrial applications (see below).

## Industrial applications

Main article: [Industrial enzymes](/wiki/Industrial_enzymes "Industrial enzymes")

Enzymes are used in the [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") and other industrial applications when extremely specific catalysts are required. Enzymes in general are limited in the number of reactions they have evolved to catalyze and also by their lack of stability in [organic solvents](/wiki/Organic_solvent "Organic solvent") and at high temperatures. As a consequence, [protein engineering](/wiki/Protein_engineering "Protein engineering") is an active area of research and involves attempts to create new enzymes with novel properties, either through rational design or *in vitro* evolution.[[115]](#cite_note-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116) These efforts have begun to be successful, and a few enzymes have now been designed "from scratch" to catalyze reactions that do not occur in nature.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

| --- | --- | --- |
| **[Biofuel industry](/wiki/Biofuel "Biofuel")** | [Cellulases](/wiki/Cellulase "Cellulase") | Break down cellulose into sugars that can be fermented to produce [cellulosic ethanol](/wiki/Cellulosic_ethanol "Cellulosic ethanol").[[118]](#cite_note-cheng-118) |
| [Ligninases](/wiki/Ligninase "Ligninase") | Pretreatment of [biomass](/wiki/Biomass "Biomass") for biofuel production.[[118]](#cite_note-cheng-118) |

| **[Brewing industry](/wiki/Brewing "Brewing")** | [Amylase](/wiki/Amylase "Amylase"), [glucanases](/wiki/Glucanase "Glucanase"), [proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") | Split polysaccharides and proteins in the [malt](/wiki/Malt "Malt").[[120]](#cite_note-briggs-120): 150–9 |
| [Betaglucanases](/wiki/Betaglucanase "Betaglucanase") | Improve the [wort](/wiki/Wort "Wort") and beer filtration characteristics.[[120]](#cite_note-briggs-120): 545 |
| [Amyloglucosidase](/wiki/Amyloglucosidase "Amyloglucosidase") and [pullulanases](/wiki/Pullulanase "Pullulanase") | Make low-calorie [beer](/wiki/Beer "Beer") and adjust fermentability.[[120]](#cite_note-briggs-120): 575 |
| [Acetolactate decarboxylase](/wiki/Acetolactate_decarboxylase "Acetolactate decarboxylase") (ALDC) | Increase fermentation efficiency by reducing [diacetyl](/wiki/Diacetyl "Diacetyl") formation.[[121]](#cite_note-121) |
| **[Culinary uses](/wiki/Cooking "Cooking")** | [Papain](/wiki/Papain "Papain") | [Tenderize](/wiki/Tenderizer "Tenderizer") meat for cooking.[[122]](#cite_note-122) |
| **[Dairy industry](/wiki/Dairy "Dairy")** | [Rennin](/wiki/Chymosin "Chymosin") | [Hydrolyze](/wiki/Hydrolyze "Hydrolyze") protein in the manufacture of [cheese](/wiki/Cheese "Cheese").[[123]](#cite_note-123) |
| [Lipases](/wiki/Lipase "Lipase") | Produce [Camembert cheese](/wiki/Camembert_cheese "Camembert cheese") and [blue cheeses](/wiki/Blue_cheese "Blue cheese") such as [Roquefort](/wiki/Roquefort "Roquefort").[[124]](#cite_note-124) |
| **[Food processing](/wiki/Food_processing "Food processing")** | [Amylases](/wiki/Amylase "Amylase") | Produce sugars from [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch"), such as in making [high-fructose corn syrup](/wiki/High-fructose_corn_syrup "High-fructose corn syrup").[[125]](#cite_note-125) |
| [Proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") | Lower the protein level of [flour](/wiki/Flour "Flour"), as in [biscuit](/wiki/Biscuit "Biscuit")-making.[[126]](#cite_note-GMOdatabase-126) |
| [Trypsin](/wiki/Trypsin "Trypsin") | Manufacture [hypoallergenic](/wiki/Hypoallergenic "Hypoallergenic") baby foods.[[126]](#cite_note-GMOdatabase-126) |
| [Cellulases](/wiki/Cellulase "Cellulase"), [pectinases](/wiki/Pectinase "Pectinase") | Clarify [fruit juices](/wiki/Fruit_juice "Fruit juice").[[127]](#cite_note-127) |
| **[Molecular biology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology")** | [Nucleases](/wiki/Nuclease "Nuclease"), [DNA ligase](/wiki/DNA_ligase "DNA ligase") and [polymerases](/wiki/Polymerase "Polymerase") | Use [restriction digestion](/wiki/Restriction_enzyme "Restriction enzyme") and the [polymerase chain reaction](/wiki/Polymerase_chain_reaction "Polymerase chain reaction") to create [recombinant DNA](/wiki/Recombinant_DNA "Recombinant DNA").[[1]](#cite_note-Stryer_2002-1): 6.2 |
| **[Paper industry](/wiki/Paper "Paper")** | [Xylanases](/wiki/Xylanase "Xylanase"), [hemicellulases](/wiki/Hemicellulase "Hemicellulase") and [lignin peroxidases](/wiki/Lignin_peroxidase "Lignin peroxidase") | Remove [lignin](/wiki/Lignin "Lignin") from [kraft pulp](/wiki/Kraft_pulp "Kraft pulp").[[128]](#cite_note-128) |
| **[Personal care](/wiki/Personal_care "Personal care")** | [Proteases](/wiki/Protease "Protease") | Remove proteins on [contact lenses](/wiki/Contact_lens "Contact lens") to prevent infections.[[129]](#cite_note-129) |
| **[Starch industry](/wiki/Starch "Starch")** | [Amylases](/wiki/Amylase "Amylase") | Convert [starch](/wiki/Starch "Starch") into [glucose](/wiki/Glucose "Glucose") and various [syrups](/wiki/Inverted_sugar_syrup "Inverted sugar syrup").[[130]](#cite_note-130) |
